# What are the influential factors for emotional regulation? A latent profile analysis based on PISA 2022 Taiwan data

[*Humanities and Social Sciences Communications*](/palcomms)
**volume 12**, Article number: 1154 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

909 Accesses

7 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Emotional regulation refers to an individual’s ability to manage and respond to negative emotional experiences and stressors in a way that promotes effective emotional functioning and coping. Adolescence is a critical period for the development of emotional regulation, during which students from different backgrounds, such as gender, grade, and economic, social, and cultural status (ESCS) may exhibit different patterns of emotional regulation development. In PISA 2022, stress resistance and emotional control were used to measure emotional regulation. Thus, the aim of the study was to examine the current state of emotional regulation among 15-year-old students in Taiwan using data from PISA 2022 database. First, the study sought to explore different level in emotional regulation across various latent classes through Latent Profile Analysis (LPA). Second, it aimed to investigate the influence of students’ background variables on these latent classes of the emotional regulation through three-step ML method. The key finding of the study are as follows: (1) There was a significant decline in emotional regulation when transitioning between learning stages. (2) Gender was the most significant factor influencing the latent classes of emotional regulation among the students’ backgrounds. (3) Limited effects of ESCS on emotional regulation were found in adolescents. Based on these findings of the study, two suggestions are provided to future education policymakers, general and counseling teachers, as well as education researchers: (1) Creating a supportive and welcoming environment, such as offering lectures and promotional activities, would be beneficial in fostering trust among students (2) It is crucial to teach students how to respect and respond to others’ emotions, regardless of whether they are male or female.

## Introduction

### The importance of emotional regulation for adolescents

Social and emotional skills encompass the abilities that enable individuals to regulate their thoughts, emotions, and behaviors, profoundly influencing various personal and societal outcomes throughout their lives (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development [OECD], [2017](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR31 "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) (2017) Social and emotional skills: Well-being, connectedness and success. OECD Publishing")). Adolescence, in particular, represents a critical developmental stage characterized by significant emotional, cognitive, neurological, and social changes, which collectively lay the foundation for the development of emotion regulation (Hollenstein and Lougheed, [2013](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR22 "Hollenstein T, Lougheed JP (2013) Beyond storm and stress typicality, transactions, timing, and temperament to account for adolescent change. Am Psychol 68:444–454. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1037/a0033586
                  
                ")). Given the substantial development of emotion regulation during this period (Young et al. [2019](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR53 "Young KS, Sandman CF, Craske MG (2019) Positive and negative emotion regulation in adolescence: links to anxiety and depression. Brain Sci 9(4):76. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/brainsci9040076
                  
                "); Silvers, [2022](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR42 "Silvers JA (2022) Adolescence as a pivotal period for emotion regulation development. Curr. Opin. Psychol. 44:258–263. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.copsyc.2021.09.023
                  
                ")) and its pivotal role in adolescent well-being and functioning (Fombouchet et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR14 "Fombouchet Y, Pineau S, Perchec C, Lucenet J, Lannegrand L (2023) The development of emotion regulation in adolescence: what do we know and where to go next? Soc Dev 32(4):1227–1242. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/sode.12684
                  
                ")), emotion regulation is recognized as a fundamental component of social and emotional learning (Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional Learning [CASEL], [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR11 "Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional Learning (CASEL) (2020) What is the CASEL framework? 
                  https://casel.org/fundamentals-of-sel/what-is-the-casel-framework/
                  
                ")). This underscores the primary importance of students’ mental health in fostering positive growth and development. Therefore, a comprehensive understanding of students’ emotional regulation is essential for supporting their overall well-being and academic success, as well as for enhancing our understanding of individual differences in mental health and adjustment during this critical period of increased risk (Silk et al., [2003](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR41 "Silk JS, Steinberg L, Morris AS (2003) Adolescents’ emotion regulation in daily life: links to depressive symptoms and problem behavior. Child Dev 74:1869–1880. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1046/j.1467-8624.2003.00643.x
                  
                ")).

Moreover, students with lower emotional regulation skills tend to report poorer well-being outcomes, including reduced life satisfaction and psychological well-being (OECD, [2024a](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR35 "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) (2024a) Social and Emotional Skills for Better Lives: Findings from the OECD Survey on Social and Emotional Skills 2023. OECD Publishing. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1787/35ca7b7c-en
                  
                ")). Emotion regulation has been identified as a key predictor of school absences (Lounsbury et al., [2004](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR26 "Lounsbury JW, Steel RP, Loveland JM, Gibson LW (2004) An investigation of personality traits in relation to adolescent school absenteeism. J Youth Adolesc 33:457–466. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1023/B:JOYO.0000037637.20329.97
                  
                ")) and negative behaviors (Tackett, [2006](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR45 "Tackett J (2006) Evaluating models of the personality-psychopathology relationship in children and adolescents. Clin Psychol. Rev. 26:584–599")). Additionally, it is closely associated with various aspects of mental health, including life satisfaction, personal well-being (Tauber et al., [2016](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR46 "Tauber B, Wahl HW, Schröder J (2016) Personality and life satisfaction over 12 years: Contrasting mid- and late life. J. Gerontopsychol Geriatr Psychiatry 29:37–48. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1024/1662-9647/a000141
                  
                "); Strickhouser et al., [2017](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR44 "Strickhouser JE, Zell E, Krizan Z (2017) Does personality predict health and well-being? A metasynthesis. Health Psychol 36(8):797. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1037/hea0000475
                  
                ")), affect, overall psychological well-being, and social relationships (Gross and John, [2003](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR19 "Gross JJ, John OP (2003) Individual differences in two emotion regulation processes: implications for affect, relationships, and well-being. J Personal Soc Psychol 85(2):348–362. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.85.2.348
                  
                ")). Given that adolescence represents a critical period of exploration and emotional development, marking the transition from childhood to adulthood (Casey et al., [2010](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR5 "Casey BJ, Duhoux S, Cohen MM (2010) Adolescence: what do transmission, transition, and translation have to do with it? Neuron 67(5):749–760. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2010.08.033
                  
                ")), academic demands, peer relationships, and social pressures can impose a considerable mental burden on students. Consequently, the ability to regulate emotions effectively poses a significant challenge for adolescents, highlighting the need for targeted interventions to support their emotional well-being.

Besides, previous studies have shown that emotional regulation varies across gender, and socioeconomic backgrounds. Findings from the 2019 OECD Survey on Social and Emotional Skills indicate that boys tend to exhibit higher levels of emotional regulation, particularly stress resistance, while socioeconomically advantaged students demonstrate stronger social and emotional skills compared to their disadvantaged peers (OECD, [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR32 "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) (2021) Beyond Academic Learning: First Results from the Survey of Social and Emotional Skills. OECD Publishing. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1787/92a11084-en
                  
                ")). Additionally, Herd et al. ([2020](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR21 "Herd T, King-Casas B, Kim-Spoon J (2020) Developmental changes in emotion regulation during adolescence: associations with socioeconomic risk and family emotional context. J Youth Adolesc 49(7):1545–1557. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10964-020-01193-2
                  
                ")) identified family socioeconomic risk as a significant factor influencing the development of emotional regulation. Despite these insights, there is limited research examining differences in emotional regulation across grade levels, highlighting a gap that warrants further investigation. According to the 2023 Taiwan High School Students’ Mental Health Survey (Child Welfare League Foundation [CWLF], [2023](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR8 "Child Welfare League Foundation (CWLF) (2023) 2023 Taiwan high school students’ mental health survey results. 
                  https://www.children.org.tw/publication_research/research_report/2544
                  
                ")) indicate that 12.2% of students experience severe or above-average stress levels. Notably, senior high school students with severe stress (16.0%) exhibit nearly twice the prevalence compared to their junior high school counterparts (8.2%). In addition, recent findings indicate that more than half of Taiwanese junior high school students experience stress regarding their future, with a growing proportion of students exhibiting emotional difficulties (Child Welfare League Foundation [CWLF], [2025](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR9 "Child Welfare League Foundation (CWLF) (2025) Child Welfare League Foundation investigation: mental health alert for Taiwan’s junior high school students- enrollment pressure and social addiction become double challenges for teenagers. 
                  https://www.children.org.tw/news/news_detail/3284
                  
                ")). These trends underscore the need to consider grade level as a key background factor in the study of emotional regulation.

### The influence of emotional regulation in Chinese culture

The values embedded in contemporary Chinese culture profoundly shape adolescent emotion regulation, particularly in relation to collectivism, academic pressure, interpersonal harmony, and cultural self-cultivation. Traditional Chinese culture prioritizes collective interests and social harmony, often at the expense of individual socio-emotional well-being, leading to insufficient professional attention to adolescents’ internalizing problems, such as depressive emotions (Chen et al., [2000](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR6 "Chen X, Chen H, Kaspar V, Noh S (2000) Adolescent social, emotional, and school adjustment in Mainland China. Int J Group Tens 29:51–78. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1005178713623
                  
                ")). In terms of parenting attitudes, for example, Taiwanese mothers place greater emphasis on obedience, emotional self-control, and face-saving culture, which results in lower acceptance of adolescents’ internalizing problems. They tend to perceive such issues as disruptive to family harmony rather than as individual psychological adjustments (Chen et al., [2024](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR7 "Chen YL, Freeman AJ, Huang KJ, Kraus SW (2024) Perceptions of youth internalizing symptoms: cross-cultural comparisons between Taiwanese and U.S. mothers. Transcultural Psychiatry, 13634615241272997. Advance online publication. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/13634615241272997
                  
                ")). As Lee ([2024](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR25 "Lee Y-C (2024) Culturally integrated perspective on emotion regulation theory: reflections on cultural fit and cross-cultural applicability. Indigenous Psychol Res Chinese Soc 62, 63–70. Retrieved from 
                  https://www.airitilibrary.com/Article/Detail?DocID=10298282-N202501170005-00002
                  
                ")) highlights that emotion regulation in Chinese culture is not solely aimed at individual psychological well-being but serves to maintain interpersonal harmony. Besides, East Asian parents set higher academic expectations for their children but report lower satisfaction with their children’s academic performance. This high-pressure environment further influences adolescents’ emotion regulation abilities (Crystal et al., [1994](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR12 "Crystal DS, Chen C, Fuligni AJ, Stevenson HW, Hsu C-C, Ko H-J, Kitamura S, Kimura S (1994) Psychological maladjustment and academic achievement: a cross-cultural study of Japanese, Chinese, and American high school students. Child Dev 65(3):738–753. 
                  https://doi.org/10.2307/1131415
                  
                ")). Studies have also indicated that maladaptive cognitions arising from academic pressure can impair adolescents’ ability to regulate emotions in social situations, particularly affecting their control over online social interactions (Chong et al., [2014](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR10 "Chong WH, Chye S, Huan VS, Ang RP (2014) Generalized problematic internet use and regulation of social emotional competence: yhe mediating role of maladaptive cognitions arising from academic expectation stress on adolescents. Comput Hum Behav 38:151–158. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2014.05.023
                  
                ")). As a result, emotion regulation in Chinese culture is a carefully calibrated process, balancing individual emotions with social expectations. These findings collectively indicate that emotion regulation within Chinese culture is profoundly influenced by traditional cultural values.

Above all, emotional regulation plays a crucial role in the well-being of adolescents. Particularly, within the context of conservative Chinese culture, students may refrain from expressing themselves in order to maintain harmony and avoid disrupting the social atmosphere, especially when faced with uncontrollable situations. However, existing studies on emotional regulation among Taiwanese students primarily focus on specific regulation strategies rather than examining their overall emotional regulation status. While cultural context significantly shapes emotional regulation, individual background characteristics such as gender, grade level, and socioeconomic status may also influence adolescents’ emotional coping abilities. Thus, the primary aim of this study was to examine the emotional regulations performance of 15-year-old Taiwanese students, particularly the gap of gender, grade, and economic, social and cultural status (ESCS). The analysis was based on data from the 2022 Programme for International Student Assessment (PISA), a large-scale international assessment developed by OECD. In PISA 2022, emotional regulation was conceptualized through two key constructs: stress resistance and emotional control. Moreover, this study had two main objectives. First, this study was to explore the latent profile analysis (LPA) model that best represents the latent classes of emotional regulation which was composed of stress resistance and emotional control. Second, this study was to examine the influence of students’ backgrounds such as gender, grade, and ESCS on the latent classes of emotional regulation. Therefore, two research questions were addressed in this study as follows:

RQ1: What are the emotional regulation profiles that characterize the stress resistance and emotional control of 15-year-old Taiwanese students?

RQ2: What is the role of 15-year-old Taiwanese students’ background factors (e.g., gender, grade, and ESCS) in predicting emotional regulation profiles?

## Literature review

### The definition and concept of emotional regulation

The study of emotional regulation can be approached from a variety of disciplinary perspectives, including biological and theoretical. From the perspective of biology, the development of frontal cortex performs a significant function in the interpretation, expression, and regulation of emotion (Fox, [1994](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR15 "Fox NA (1994) Dynamic cerebral processes underlying emotion regulation. Monogr Soc Res Child Dev 59(2/3):152–166. 
                  https://doi.org/10.2307/1166143
                  
                "); Alipour et al., [2011](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR1 "Alipour A, Arefnasab Z, Babamahmoodi A (2011) Emotional intelligence and prefrontal cortex: a comparative study based on Wisconsin card sorting test (WCST). Iran J Psychiatry Behav Sci 5(2):114")). The enhancement of emotional regulation was enabled by augmented interconnectivity between regions that are pivotal for the processing of emotional and social information (e.g., the amygdala, ventral striatum, orbitofrontal cortex, medial prefrontal cortex, and superior temporal sulcus) and regions that are indispensable for cognitive control (e.g., the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, anterior and posterior cingulate, and temporo-parietal cortices) (Steinberg, [2008](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR43 "Steinberg L (2008) A social neuroscience perspective on adolescent risk-taking. In Biosocial theories of crime. Routledge, pp 435–463. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dr.2007.08.002
                  
                ")). From a theoretical perspective, Gross ([1998](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR18 "Gross JJ (1998) Antecedent-and response-focused emotion regulation: divergent consequences for experience, expression, and physiology. J Personal Soc Psychol 74(1):224–237")) distinguishes between antecedent-focused strategies, such as reappraisal, and response-focused strategies, such as expressive suppression, emphasizing the importance of emotional awareness, acceptance, and regulation, along with the application of appropriate strategies to meet personal and situational demands (Gratz and Roemer, [2004](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR17 "Gratz KL, Roemer L (2004) Multidimensional assessment of emotion regulation and dysregulation: development, factor structure, and initial validation of the difficulties in emotion regulation scale. J Psychopathol Behav Assess 26(1):41–54")). Consequently, individuals’ emotional self-regulation, shaped by evolving appraisals, goals, and contextual factors, adapts to complex biological and environmental risks, balancing immediate and long-term goals within the context of psychopathology (Thompson and Goodman, [2010](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR48 "Thompson RA, Goodman M (2010) Development of emotion regulation: more than meets the eye. In: AM Kring & DM Sloan (eds) Emotion regulation and psychopathology: a transdiagnostic approach to etiology and treatment. The Guilford Press, pp 38–58")). Thus, emotional regulation is the term used to describe the capacity of an individual to manage negative emotional experiences and stressors, in a manner that allows for the effective functioning of emotional management (Kankaraš and Suarez-Alvarez, [2019](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR23 "Kankaraš M, Suarez-Alvarez J (2019) Assessment framework of the OECD study on social and emotional skills. OECD Education Working Papers, 207. OECD Publishing. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1787/5007adef-en
                  
                ")).

Therefore, emotional regulation is regarded as both an ability and a process through which individuals manage their emotions in response to negative experiences and stressors. Understanding the emotional regulation performance of 15-year-old students is a key focus of this study. To frame this investigation, the study adopts the definition of emotional regulation proposed by PISA 2022, which describes it as “the processes through which individuals influence the emotions they have, when they have them, and how they experience and express them” (Gross, [1998](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR18 "Gross JJ (1998) Antecedent-and response-focused emotion regulation: divergent consequences for experience, expression, and physiology. J Personal Soc Psychol 74(1):224–237")) to enhance, mitigate, or sustain these emotions (Usán Supervía and Quílez Robres, [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR50 "Usán Supervía P, Quílez Robres A (2021) Emotional regulation and academic performance in the academic context: the mediating role of self-efficacy in secondary education students. Int J Environ Res Public Health 18(11):5715. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph18115715
                  
                ")) (OECD, [2024b](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR36 "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) (2024b) PISA 2022 Results (Volume V): Learning Strategies and Attitudes for Life. PISA, OECD Publishing. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1787/c2e44201-en
                  
                ")).

### The role of students’ background on emotional regulation

This study examined three key student background factors—gender, grade level, and economic, social, and cultural status (ESCS)—to explore their potential impact on emotional regulation. However, most existing research on this topic has focused on emotional regulation in terms of strategies rather than as a broader ability, process or function (Fombouchet et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR14 "Fombouchet Y, Pineau S, Perchec C, Lucenet J, Lannegrand L (2023) The development of emotion regulation in adolescence: what do we know and where to go next? Soc Dev 32(4):1227–1242. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/sode.12684
                  
                ")).

Gender differences in emotional regulation strategies have been widely discussed in previous research. Studies have consistently shown that females are more likely to engage in social support-seeking and rumination, whereas males tend to exhibit greater tendencies toward passivity, avoidance, and emotional suppression (Santos et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR40 "Santos AC, Simões C, Cefai C, Freitas E, Arriaga P (2021) Emotion regulation and student engagement: age and gender differences during adolescence. Int J Educ Res 109:101830. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijer.2021.101830
                  
                "); Zimmermann and Iwanski, [2014](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR55 "Zimmermann P, Iwanski A (2014) Emotion regulation from early adolescence to emerging adulthood and middle adulthood: age differences, gender differences, and emotion-specific developmental variations. Int J Behav Dev 38(2):182–194. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0165025413515405
                  
                "); Blanchard-Fields and Coats, [2008](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR4 "Blanchard-Fields F, Coats A (2008) The experience of anger and sadness in everyday problems impacts age differences in emotion regulation. Dev Psychol 44:1547–1556. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1037/a0013915
                  
                "); Silk et al., [2003](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR41 "Silk JS, Steinberg L, Morris AS (2003) Adolescents’ emotion regulation in daily life: links to depressive symptoms and problem behavior. Child Dev 74:1869–1880. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1046/j.1467-8624.2003.00643.x
                  
                ")). These differences may reflect culturally shaped gender roles and socialization processes (Saarni et al., [2007](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR38 "Saarni C, Campos JJ, Camras LA, Witherington D (2007) Emotional development: Action, communication, and understanding. In: Eisenberg N (ed) Handbook of child psychology. Vol. 3: Social, emotional, and personality development. New York, NY: Wiley, pp 226–299")). For example, Sanchis-Sanchis et al. ([2020](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR39 "Sanchis-Sanchis A, Grau MD, Moliner AR, Morales-Murillo CP (2020) Effects of age and gender in emotion regulation of children and adolescents. Front Psychol 11:946. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2020.00946
                  
                ")) found that girls aged 13–16 scored higher than boys in their use of strategies to regulate sadness, anxiety, and anger. On the other hand, some cross-cultural studies have challenged the consistency of these patterns. Yusoff et al. ([2024](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR54 "Yusoff SR, Hoesni SM, Muhammad NA (2024) Emotion regulation: an exploration of gender differences among adolescents in Malaysia. e-BANGI J 21(1). 
                  https://doi.org/10.17576/ebangi.2024.2101.34
                  
                ")), in a Malaysian context, found no significant gender differences in the use of cognitive reappraisal or expressive suppression among adolescents, suggesting that cultural norms may mediate gendered expressions of emotional regulation. These mixed findings imply that although gender may influence emotional regulation strategies, its relationship with overall emotional regulation ability remains complex and context-dependent. Besides, while females are often assumed to have better emotional regulation due to greater use of adaptive strategies, some findings suggest the contrary—especially during adolescence. For instance, the mid-adolescent group (ages 13–15) was found to use fewer adaptive strategies compared to younger and older peers, with girls in this age range reporting increased emotional difficulties (Laursen et al., [1998](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR24 "Laursen B, Coy KC, Collins WA (1998) Reconsidering changes in parent-child conflict across adolescence: a meta-analysis. Child Dev 69(3):817–832"); Zimmermann and Iwanski, [2014](/articles/s41599-025-05179-y#ref-CR55 "Zimmermann P, Iwanski A (2014) Emotion regulation from early adolescence to emerging adulthood and middle adulthood: age differences, gender differences, and emotion-specific developmental variations. Int J Behav Dev 38(2):182–194. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0165025413515405
                  
                ")). These findings are particularly relevant to the present study, as results indicate that female students were more likely to fall into lower emotional regulation profiles, possibly due to increased sensitivity and maladaptive rumination during periods of stress.

Moreover, there is a lack of research specifically examining emotional regulation in relation to adolescents’ grade levels. Most existing studies address this issue through the lens of age or developmental stages, focusing on differences between early and late adolescence rather than formal educational grade distinctions. --- SOURCE 2: Regulating others' emotions: An exploratory study of everyday ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478

SUMMARY:
Recent research showed that ability emotional intelligence is related to higher scores on global questionnaire measures of reframing, receptive listening, and valuing extrinsic regulation strategies, but not humor or distraction (Xiao et al., 2022). On each occasion that participants reported regulating another person's emotions, they were asked to rate how much they used each of the five extrinsic emotion regulation strategies: humor (make them laugh, tell them something funny), distraction (help them think about something else, distract them from their problem), cognitive reframing (help them to see the situation in a different way, help them to see the positive side), receptive listening (listen to what they wanted to tell you, let them talk about their issues), and valuing (make them feel special or cared about, tell them how much you value them).

Abstract
--------

Emotion regulation predicts important life outcomes including psychopathology, social outcomes, work performance, and academic achievement. Extrinsic [emotion regulation strategies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/emotion-regulation-strategy "Learn more about emotion regulation strategies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (the strategies used to influence others' emotions) are under-studied relative to intrinsic regulation strategies (the strategies used to regulate one's own emotions). In the current study, we used experience sampling to examine participants' extrinsic emotion regulation strategies. Participants (165 university students) reported the strategies they used to regulate others' emotions on 30 occasions over 5 days. The findings suggest that participants frequently report regulating others' emotions and report using receptive listening and reframing more than humor and distraction. Empathy, conscientiousness and agreeableness were associated with differences in the use of extrinsic emotion regulation strategies. These findings help characterize everyday extrinsic emotion regulation strategies and how these strategies relate to other individual differences.

1. Introduction
---------------

Emotion regulation refers to people's attempts to control the duration, type and intensity of emotions that they or others experience ([Gross, 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0070)). While there is a large amount of research on the strategies people use to regulate their own emotions (intrinsic emotion regulation), very little is known about the strategies people use to regulate others' emotions (extrinsic emotion regulation) in daily life. In the current study, we address this research gap by examining extrinsic [emotion regulation strategies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/emotion-regulation-strategy "Learn more about emotion regulation strategies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") using an experience sampling method (ESM). This approach allows us to address several key research questions: a) how often people engage in extrinsic regulation attempts; b) which strategies they use most often and on whom, and c) the demographic and psychological characteristics associated with using these strategies.

### 1.1. Extrinsic emotion regulation

Despite the ubiquitous social nature of emotion regulation, [Campos et al. (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb3000) found that as few as 12% of emotion regulation studies assessed regulation in the presence of another person. That is, although there is a large and exponentially growing research literature on emotion regulation, most of it is based on how people regulate their own emotions without support from others (_intrinsic intrapersonal emotion regulation_). In contrast, it is only recently that there has been a focus on how people regulate their own emotions via interacting with others (_intrinsic interpersonal emotion regulation_) or how people regulate the emotions of others (_extrinsic emotion regulation_) ([Nozaki & Mikolajczak, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0135); [Zaki & Williams, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0215)). See [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#f0005) for an illustration of how the goal of regulation (one's own versus others' emotions) and means of regulation (social versus non-social) determine whether the regulation is intrinsic intrapersonal, intrinsic interpersonal or extrinsic.

Extrinsic emotion regulation is defined as “an action performed with the goal of influencing another person's emotion trajectory; it can aim to decrease or increase either negative emotion or positive emotion” ([Nozaki & Mikolajczak, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0135), p. 10). The current study focuses only on affect improving (increasing positive emotion or decreasing negative emotion).

### 1.2. How often do people engage in extrinsic emotion regulation?

Using experience sampling data, [Geiger et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0050) found that people aimed to improve others' emotions in 46% of social interactions, and aimed to worsen others' affect in 15% of social interactions. Participants were predominantly regulating a friend (91% to 92% of the time), or a romantic partner (8% to 9% of the time). [Tran et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0185) examined how often people engage in extrinsic emotion regulation over 7 days, using both daily diary data (Study 1) and experience sampling data (Study 2). Most people engaged in extrinsic regulation at least once over the week (82% and 92% for Study 1 versus 2), and on any one occasion a third to a half of people reported engaging in extrinsic emotion regulation on any one occasion (36% to 47% for Study 1 versus 2). Most of the time (97% to 98% of occasions) participants were trying to improve the other person's emotion rather than worsen it.

While prior ESM studies examined whether people engage in extrinsic emotion regulation in daily life, to our knowledge no ESM research has yet examined what people actually do—the _strategies_ people use to regulate others' emotions. This is the primary goal of the current study.

### 1.3. Which emotion regulation strategies do people use?

In one of the first studies to distinguish different extrinsic emotion regulation strategies, [Niven et al. (2009)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0125) used questionnaires, diary studies, and a card sorting task to produce the _Taxonomy of Controlled Extrinsic Regulation Strategies._ The taxonomy included six strategies for improving others' emotions (cognitive engagement, problem-focused engagement, distracting, humor, valuing, target-focused engagement). Since this starting point, several research teams have proposed and examined the different strategies that people might use to improve others' emotions (or receive from others to regulate their own emotions). The rapid growth of research, particularly in the last two years, has meant that research teams are often working in parallel, using different labels for conceptually similar strategies. In [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#t0005), we have identified and defined a common set of strategies from 11 different research teams, conceptually mapping the different terminology from different studies on to this common core ([Little et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0085); [Nils & Rimé, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0120); [Niven et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0125); [Nozaki & Mikolajczak, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0140); [Pauw et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0150); [Ruan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0165); [Xiao et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0200)). There are eight strategies that are common to multiple frameworks, and the current study will examine five of them: _[cognitive reframing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/cognitive-reframing "Learn more about cognitive reframing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_, _receptive listening_, _distraction_, _humor_, and _valuing_.

While extrinsic regulation strategies have not yet been examined using the experience sampling method, [Ruan et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0165) used the daily diary method to examine seven affect-improving strategies in a sample of romantic couples (receptive listening, cognitive reframing, direct action, distraction, situation selection, savoring, and expressive suppression). They reported that expressive suppression was used much less frequently than the other strategies (on 11% of days compared to 80% for savoring and 41% to 52% for the other strategies). Only receptive listening and direct situation modification were effective in regulating a partner's emotions (as determined by significant partner effects of each strategy on effectiveness ratings). They found that while people frequently regulate their romantic partner's emotions, there was little evidence of a relationship between strategy use and relationship satisfaction.

### 1.4. Who uses these strategies?

Below we briefly discuss the individual differences factors that are explored in the current study.

#### 1.4.1. Empathy

Broadly speaking, empathy is the extent to which an individual mirrors the emotional experience of others ([Zaki, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0205)). Empathy is a strong predictor of extrinsic emotion regulation ([Zaki, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0205)). For example, empathy predicts receptive listening among romantic couples ([Devoldre et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0035)). In particular, empathy plays a key role in shaping the goals individuals form about others' emotions. As empathetic individuals are likely to share the [affective state](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/emotion "Learn more about affective state from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of others, these goals can both be both pro-socially motivated (I want my friend to feel better) or pragmatically motivated (I want my friend to feel better, so that I feel better; [Zaki, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0205)). [Geiger et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0050) found that both state and trait empathy predicted whether people formed goals to make others feel better in daily life (though they did not examine the strategies used). However, [Tanna and MacCann (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0180) found that situation-specific empathy for the target person was significantly correlated with greater use of distraction, humor, cognitive reframing and receptive listening to regulate the target's emotions, with the strongest association with receptive listening (_r_=0.56) and the weakest association for distraction (_r_=0.19).

#### 1.4.2. Emotional intelligence

Emotional intelligence is the ability to process, use, and modify emotional information ([MacCann et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0100)). There is substantial theoretical overlap between the construct of emotional intelligence and emotion regulation ([Grewal et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0065); [Mayer & Salovey, 1995](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0110)). Previous research supports the idea that emotional intelligence is a robust predictor of coping strategies ([Goldenberg et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0060); [MacCann et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0095)). Specifically, emotional intelligence seems to positively predict adaptive coping strategies, and negatively predict [maladaptive coping strategies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/maladaptive-coping-strategy "Learn more about maladaptive coping strategies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Gohm et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0055); [Goldenberg et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0060)). Recent research showed that ability emotional intelligence is related to higher scores on global questionnaire measures of reframing, receptive listening, and valuing extrinsic regulation strategies, but not humor or distraction ([Xiao et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0200)). Effects were larger for receptive listening than reframing or valuing, and larger for emotion management than for [emotion understanding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/emotion-understanding "Learn more about emotion understanding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

#### 1.4.3. Self-esteem

Self-esteem refers to “the extent to which one prizes, values, approves of, or likes oneself” ([Blascovich et al., 1991](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0015), p. 115). Self-esteem has been linked to individuals' expectations of affect, with high self-esteem individuals expecting more positive affect and low self-esteem individuals being more accepting of negative affect and their inability to modulate it ([Wood et al., 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0195)). High self-esteem individuals tend to use more adaptive regulatory strategies such as reframing ([De Castella et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0030)), while low self-esteem has been linked to an inability to regulate one's emotions ([Garofalo et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb0045)). Note, that the above evidence is drawn from literature examining the relationship between self-esteem and intrinsic emotion regulation. To our knowledge, there are no previous studies examining the link between self-esteem and extrinsic emotion regulation and we thus base our predictions on the research on intrinsic regulation.

#### 1.4.4. Big-5 personality

The relationship between Big-5 personality factors and intrinsic emotion regulation has received considerable research attention. A meta-analysis by [Barańczuk (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924001478#bb3005) found that [Big Five personality traits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/five-factor-model-of-personality "Learn more about Big Five personality traits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") predict both adaptive and maladaptive regulation strategies. Specifically, extraversion, openness to experience, agreeableness, and conscientiousness were positively related to adaptive emotion regulation strategies and negatively related to maladaptive regulation strategies, while [neuroticism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/neuroticism "Learn more about neuroticism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") showed the reverse pattern. As of yet, the relationship between Big-5 personality and daily use of extrinsic emotion regulation strategies has not been explored.

Emotional regulation strategies in daily life: the intensity of emotions and regulation choice

Abstract
--------

### Objective

Emotion regulation is an adaptive ability affecting people’s physical and mental health, quality of life and functioning. In the present study we focused on the influence of the intensity of experienced emotions on emotion regulation strategies (ERS) that are applied in everyday life.

### Methods

For 7 days the participants kept an online diary where every day they described the situation which had aroused their strongest negative emotions. Next, they identified the emotions, their intensity and the type of applied strategies (acceptance vs. reappraisal vs. rumination vs. distraction vs. suppression). The study involved 88 people _N_ = 88, which gives 538 observations.

### Results

The obtained results indicate that the intensity of emotions affects the choice of regulation strategies. When the intensity increases, people are more likely to choose the rumination strategy and less likely to choose the reappraisal strategy. However, the expected relationship between the intensity and the number of regulation strategies was not confirmed. In turn, it was gender (male) that turned out to be associated with a greater number of strategies used.

### Conclusion

The concern of this research was to look at making regulatory decisions in personally relevant and complex everyday situations. Although the emotions experienced in response to a difficult situation were varied, the intensity of the emotional experience was an important factor determining the choice of a regulation strategy. It indicates that this emotional dimension is a basic and determining aspect in people’s regulatory capabilities. These results also indicate that perhaps men in a situation perceived as stressful and worthy of emotional involvement use more regulatory strategies than women. These findings may find an application in all kinds of psychological interventions (e.g., psychotherapy, anger management therapies).

**Keywords:** emotion regulation strategies, rumination, reappraisal, acceptance, distraction, suppression, intensity of emotions, daily diary study

1. Introduction
---------------

Emotions are one of the crucial psychophysiological processes which help people adapt to the environment and achieve their goals ([Eisenberg, 2000](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B19)). Rapidly occurring social changes and the development of digital technology, which can be observed in recent years, confront people with unprecedented multitude and diversity of stimuli. It generates cognitive strain, situational stress and carries negative affective states. Over the last 2 years it was the COVID-19 pandemic and concurrent restrictions that were the particular source of a long-term threat. Due to such changes, the ability to flexibly regulate emotional reactions has become something even more valuable than before.

Emotion regulation is connected with physical ([Sapolsky, 2007](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B60)) and mental ([Gross and Muñoz, 1995](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B26)) health. Effective regulation is a factor restraining depression, correlating with work efficiency ([Diefendorff et al., 2000](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B13)), relationships satisfaction ([Rick et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B57)) and commonly perceived wellbeing ([Garnefski et al., 2001](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B22); [Gross and John, 2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B25)). Even though the issue of emotion regulation is a relatively new field of scientific studies, a number of research, mainly of the questionnaire ([Garnefski et al., 2001](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B22); [Gross and John, 2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B25); [Aldao and Nolen-Hoeksema, 2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B1)) or experimental ([Butler et al., 2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B10); [Webb et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B78); [Gross and Thompson, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B27)) character, have already been conducted. In the latter type of research the participants were usually instructed to apply one specific regulation strategy in response to an unpleasant stimulus. It allowed making a relative comparison of particular ERS ([Webb et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B78)). However, such a procedure does not reflect real situations. People usually experience a mix of emotions which vary both when it comes to their content and intensity. Also, emotion regulation is a spontaneous process. The newest research results indicate that people who have a free choice usually apply a lot of ERS ([Ehring et al., 2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B18); [Aldao and Nolen-Hoeksema, 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B2); [Szasz et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B71)). In the research conducted by [Opitz et al. (2015)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B55), in spite of the instruction telling the participants to employ the reappraisal strategy, between 50 and 75% of them interchangeably or additionally used other strategies. Studies also show that not all strategies are applied with the same frequency ([Dixon-Gordon et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B16)). Scholars are currently paying more attention to the significance of contextual factors which influence the selection and effectiveness of ERS, i.a. because of the need to verify the obtained results by means of more ecological validity methods ([Mitchell et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B49)). As a result, more and more studies are being conducted by the use of the longitudinal, daily diary or momentary report method. Such studies refer to their participants’ everyday experiences. In our study we focused on the influence of one of the most basic dimensions of emotions, namely their intensity, on the selection of a regulation strategy. In order to verify the results of laboratory research in this area, we applied the more ecological daily diary method, according to which the participants described their everyday experiences and their emotional reactions in unpleasant situations.

### 1.1. Emotion regulation strategies

People normally modulate their reactions by means of a wide range of regulation strategies ([Gross and Thompson, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B27)). Emotion regulation is defined as “the processes by which individuals influence which emotions they have, when they have them, and how they experience and express them” ([Gross et al., 1998](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B28)) (p. 275). The regulation can be introduced at any stage of the emotional process, either before or after the occurrence of the emotional response. It differentiates anticipative strategies and strategies related to the emotional response itself. According to the Gross’s model, there are five ERS groups: situation selection, situation modification, attentional deployment, cognitive change and response modulation ([Gross, 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B23)). The study presented below focused on five regulation strategies: reappraisal, rumination, distraction, suppression and acceptance. The selection was based, _inter alia_, on the results of the meta-analysis of ERS effectiveness ([Webb et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B78)).

The emotional process is initiated with attention deployment which is essentially based on directing attention in order to modify one’s emotional state ([Gross and Thompson, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B27)). Distraction is the strategy thanks to which an individual focuses their attention on different aspects of a given situation or distracts attention from it. It can also involve a change of one’s inner direction, for instance when people recall thoughts or memories which are in discrepancy with an undesirable emotional state ([Gross and Thompson, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B27)). Distraction results effective mostly in situations when it is connected with a conscious effort, not merely with deflection caused by other stimuli or a task given by the researchers ([Locke and Latham, 1990](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B42); [Webb et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B78)). The rumination strategy, which is based on concentration on negative situations, emotions and thoughts triggered by them, has the opposite effect since it results in the sustainment of negative emotions, increasing their intensity and prolonging their duration. It is also connected with increased depressive susceptibility ([Morrow and Nolen-Hoeksema, 1990](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B50)). The distraction strategy is perceived as shifting one’s attention to a positive or neutral thought, or focusing one’s attention on a different activity. The rumination strategy is perceived as concentrating one’s attention on thoughts and feelings concerning an unpleasant situation or analysing its causes and consequences. The most extensive category of mental regulation techniques is the cognitive change. It is defined as a change of thinking about a given situation or the opportunities of handling it ([Gross and Thompson, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B27)). Just like the attention shift, the cognitive change is one of the anticipative strategies applied when a situation triggering negative emotions cannot be avoided or changed. Mental attitude becomes an effective way to change one’s emotional state then. However, tasks used in research on the cognitive change are extremely varied. They concern different aspects and ways of perceiving an emotional experience. We can distinguish those ones which encouraged reassessing a given situation and detecting its positive consequences, and those which led to adopting another perspective, for instance the observer’s perspective, or reappraising the emotional experience itself ([Webb et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B78)). Some scholars also employed the combination of the above mentioned strategies. We focused on the cognitive change strategy considered as reappraisal, that is assigning a different meaning to a given situation or taking the perspective of a different person.

Nowadays the interactions based on mindfulness, enriched with the diversity of therapeutical approaches, are becoming more and more popular, for example in ACT ([Hayes et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B30)), DBT ([Linehan et al., 1994](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B41)), MBCT ([Segal et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B64)). They put special emphasis on the role of acceptance in the regulation of the emotional process. Applying it in everyday life is connected with lowering the negative affect ([Kashdan et al., 2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B32); [Shallcross et al., 2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B65); [Wojnarowska et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B80)) and physiological reactivity in response to a negative stimulus triggering negative emotions ([Troy et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B75)). Some scholars perceive this strategy as the cognitive change, the reappraisal of the emotional state, since people usually assess their negative emotional condition and do not approve it. In our study we considered the acceptance strategy as acknowledging a given situation, oneself and one’s own emotional state, accepting them the way they were without assessing them.

Suppression is the strategy based on modulating the emotional response which has already occurred, and inhibiting emotion expressing behavior. It can refer merely to supressing emotional expression, but also to feelings or thoughts regarding a given situation. The suppression of emotional expression resulted effective in lowering the intensity of the emotional response. Nonetheless, the suppression of experienced emotions and thoughts resulted to have the opposite effect or even intensify physiological reactions of one’s organism ([Gross and John, 2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B25)). The research on applying the suppression strategy revealed that the ability to use both expression and suppression of emotions is important in maintaining mental health. Whereas the frequency of supressing emotions is linked with inefficient adoption, the ability to adopt emotional expression to a given situation brings positive clinical and social results ([Bonanno et al., 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B6); [Gupta and Bonanno, 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B29)). In the present study suppression is understood as supressing thoughts, feelings and emotional expression.

In the above mentioned experimental studies, the research procedure usually imposed on the participants the choice of a specific strategy. The spontaneous choice of a regulation strategy depends on different internal and external aspects, for instance those connected with individual differences or the situational ones, resulting from the experienced emotion itself.

### 1.2. Intensity of emotions and selection of ERS

Intensity of a given emotion is one of its most characteristic features. It exerts an influence on people’s motivation and behavior. Emotional intensity is reflected in a subjective experience, but also in the form of mimic, visceral and nervous reactions ([Lang et al., 1993](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B36); [Sonnemans and Frijda, 1995](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B69)). It is a variable feature and often does not reach the maximum level. Just like in the case of the emotion itself, its duration is limited. Variations on the emotional activity scale are regarded to be relatively independent from the type of an experienced emotion, its indication (positive or negative) or content ([Diener and Iran-Nejad, 1986](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B14); [Schimmack and Diener, 1997](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B63)). What seems to be an important determinant of emotional intensity is the motivational meaning of a given situation and its influence on goals established by an individual ([Lazarus, 1991](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B37); [Sonnemans and Frijda, 1995](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B69); [Brehm, 1999](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B8); [Scherer, 1999](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B62)). Regulation changes in the emotional process occur mainly within intensity (decrease/increase) and temporal features such as the moment the emotion appears or its persistence ([Thompson, 1990](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B74)).

Recent studies indicate that emotional intensity exerts an influence on the selection of applied regulation strategies ([Suri et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B70)). More intensive negative emotions require people to apply a higher number of regulation strategies ([Barrett et al., 2000](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B3); [Gross and Thompson, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B27); [Dixon-Gordon et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B16)). It may result from a higher motivation for change, and consequently the effort put into regulation in case of experiencing an unpleasant emotional state. Studies also point out that desadaptive strategies such as avoiding are more often chosen when emotional intensity is higher (e.g., [Sheppes et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B68), [2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B67); [Sheppes and Levin, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B66)). A series of comparative experimental studies, regarding the selection of either distraction or reappraisal while being presented a stimulus triggering low or high emotion intensity, indicate that people who have a free choice prefer to apply reappraisal when the emotion intensity is low, and distraction when the emotion intensity is high ([Sheppes et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B68), [2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B67)). The same pattern was observed in relation to various stimuli (negative pictures, electrocuting; [Sheppes et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B68)) and in the study where the participants were offered a financial reward for applying the less preferable strategy ([Sheppes et al., 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B67)). Such results probably arise from a difference in the undertaken cognitive effort aiming at changing the emotional arousal. Distraction, as the strategy based on backing out and lack of engagement, requires less cognitive resources than reappraisal. Studies indicate that while making decisions people tend to minimize cognitive effort ([Kool et al., 2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B35); [Tversky and Kahneman, 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B76)), especially in situations connected with a high level of stress and danger ([Muraven and Baumeister, 2000](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B52)).

There are not many pieces of research which were conducted in natural conditions and concerned the meaning of emotion intensity in applying regulation strategies. Nevertheless, their results indicate that the use of ER strategies is more complex in daily life than in a laboratory. [Lennarz et al.’s (2019)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B38) study involving the momentary report method, conducted with the participation of a group of adolescents, demonstrated that in case of low intensity negative emotions teenagers more often applied the acceptance strategy, whereas in case of high intensity negative emotions they more frequently employed the suppression, distraction and rumination strategies. There was no significant relationship between reappraisal and emotional intensity in the study. Adult studies indicate that reappraisal is used more often when emotional intensity is lower ([Troy et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B75); [Mehta et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B47); [Wilms et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B79); [Blanke et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B5)) however, in [Ortner and Pennekamp (2020)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B56) research it was unrelated, whereas rumination is used more often when emotional intensity is higher ([Ortner and Pennekamp, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B56); [Wilms et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B79); [Blanke et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B5)). The use of distraction was associated with a higher intensity of negative emotions compared to reappraisal ([Mehta et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B47)) which was unrelated ([Wilms et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B79); [Blanke et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B5)) or less frequently used in other studies ([Ortner and Pennekamp, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B56)). There is not much research on the remaining two strategies. Acceptance was used less frequently when stressors were more intense ([Blanke et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B5)). Expressive suppression was negatively associated with experience intensity ([Ortner and Pennekamp, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B56)).

Currently available research results imply that emotion intensity influences the selection of ERS. However, the disproportion between laboratory tests and surveys carried out in everyday life on the ERS choice is still very large. The results obtained in ecological conditions are valid compared to those provided by laboratory tests. Therefore, both approaches should be treated as complementary. Furthermore, the already conducted experience sampling studies on the impact of emotions intensity on the choice of regulation strategies differ in the methodology used and the results obtained. This indicates the need for further research in this area and the replication of results by the use of different methodologies.

### 1.3. Aim and hypotheses

The aim of the study was to determine the influence of experienced emotions and their intensity on the selection of ERS. In order to ensure the ecological validity character of the procedure, the daily diary method was employed. For 1 week the participants every day described the situation which triggered their strongest unpleasant emotions. They entered their emotions independently and then assessed their intensity. Therefore, they could fully report their emotional state without the risk that it did not fit into one of the given categories. They also answered the question which regulation strategies they had applied in these situations.

The research results indicate that emotion intensity can influence the selection of specific regulation strategies ([Sheppes et al., 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B67); [Wilms et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B79); [Blanke et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B5)). Due to the fact that strong arousal concentrates attention and cognitive resources on the stimulus provoking the emotional response ([Mather and Sutherland, 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B46); [Markovic et al., 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B45)) we suppose that high emotion intensity will increase the likelihood of selecting the rumination strategy. Strong arousal does not favor the cognitive emotion regulation though ([Veilleux et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B77)). Therefore, we believe that the higher the emotion intensity, the less frequently the reappraisal strategy will be applied. From the cognitive point of view, the indications of the reappraisal strategy are the most varied ones. It is the most extensive category of regulation strategies, the determinant of which is the reappraisal of an emotional experience. The described aspects are therefore connected with various levels of cognitive effort and the complexity of cognitive mechanisms.

In accordance with the foregoing research results ([Gross and Thompson, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B27); [Dixon-Gordon et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10460911/#B16)), we assume that higher intensity of emotions will be associated with the use of more strategies.

> Hypothesis 1 (H1). The higher the emotion intensity, the more likely it is for the rumination strategy to be chosen.

> Hypothesis 2 (H2). The higher the emotion intensity, the less likely it is for the reappraisal strategy to be chosen.

> Hypothesis 3 (H3). The higher the emotion intensity, the more regulation strategies will be employed.

The study involved 180 adults. The participants were recruited by method of snowball sampling. What Is Emotional Regulation? A Definition
------------------------------------------

Emotional regulation is a dynamic and multifaceted process by which we experience and express our emotions (Thompson et al., 2008). It can be conscious, such as actively deciding to calm yourself down after a stressful meeting, or unconscious, such as automatically feeling relief after a deep breath (McRae & Gross, 2020).

Essentially, emotional regulation helps to maintain balance, ensuring that emotions do not overwhelm or disrupt our day-to-day functioning (McRae & Gross, 2020).

Effective emotional regulation enables us to respond appropriately to life’s challenges without becoming overwhelmed or acting impulsively. It promotes resilience and mental health, while difficulties with regulation can lead to various challenges that can have a profound effect on our sense of wellbeing, our relationships, and our ability to function in society (Iwakabe et al., 2023).

Emotion regulation is therefore likely to be a key [focus in therapy](https://positivepsychology.com/emotionally-focused-therapy/), and understanding the process and ways to improve it is a foundational requirement for you as a therapist.

### 5 examples of healthy emotional self-regulation

The above gives us an idea of what emotional regulation is, but what we wanted to know was, “What does that look like?” This is what we’ve learned:

Healthy emotional regulation is built on a foundation of three key factors (Naragon-Gainey et al., 2017):

*   Being able to disengage from the intensity of the emotion
*   Being able to interrupt the cycle of rumination
*   Being able to reengage with the situation adaptively

The following are some examples of healthy emotional regulation that you can look for and encourage in your clients:

1.   _Reframing negative thoughts_

 When faced with a challenging situation, rather than catastrophizing, reframe the event to focus on the learning opportunity or [silver lining](https://positivepsychology.com/find-a-silver-lining/) (Hu et al., 2014).
2.   _Acceptance and mindfulness_

 In moments of intense stress, practice acceptance and mindfulness to calm the nervous system and regain emotional equilibrium (Naragon-Gainey et al., 2017).
3.   _Take a break_

 Recognize when an emotion such as [anger is escalating](https://positivepsychology.com/anger-management-therapy/). Temporarily remove yourself from the situation to cool down before responding.
4.   _Express emotions constructively_

 Use calm, [assertive communication](https://positivepsychology.com/assertive-communication/) to express feelings appropriately instead of bottling them up or lashing out.
5.   _Problem-solving_

 Instead of ruminating on negative feelings, identify actionable steps to address the root cause of the emotion.

The use of these strategies demonstrates your client’s self-awareness and a proactive approach to managing emotions in healthy and adaptive ways.

How to Regulate Emotions: 4 Theories
------------------------------------

![Image 4: How to regulate emotions](blob:http://localhost/7e3f3d6cf00040eb687cf88440ff3856)Although the idea of emotion regulation is not new, developments in psychology have added new perspectives and hope.

[Neuroplasticity](https://positivepsychology.com/neuroplasticity/) research, for example, has taught us that we can essentially rewire our brain to better manage our emotions (Zilverstand et al., 2017).

[Somatic psychology](https://positivepsychology.com/somatic-experiencing/) research has revealed that the parasympathetic nervous system can be actively engaged after trauma and stress to help improve emotion regulation (Price & Hooven, 2018).

Here are a few key theories on how to regulate emotions.

### 1. The dual-process theory

This theory suggests that emotional regulation involves both automatic and controlled processes (Grecucci et al., 2020). Automatic regulation happens without conscious thought, while controlled regulation is a deliberate attempt to manage emotions.

### 2. The polyvagal theory

[Polyvagal theory](https://positivepsychology.com/polyvagal-theory/) focuses on the role of the autonomic nervous system (especially the vagus nerve) in emotional regulation (Porges, 1997). The theory posits that social engagement and [self-soothing](https://positivepsychology.com/self-soothing/) are key for maintaining emotional balance.

### 3. Gross’s process model of emotion regulation

This model breaks down emotion regulation into five stages: situation selection, situation modification, attentional deployment, cognitive change, and response modulation (Gross, 2015). It emphasizes that emotion regulation occurs at different points in the emotion-generating process.

### 4. The cognitive-behavioral model

This approach highlights the role of thought patterns in regulating emotions (Pruessner et al., 2020). By altering negative or [distorted thoughts](https://positivepsychology.com/cognitive-distortions/), individuals can change their emotional responses.

These theories offer a deeper understanding of the nature of regulation, from automatic physiological responses to conscious cognitive strategies. All these theories involve neuroscience, so let’s take a deeper look at what this entails.

### The neuroscience of self-regulation

While emotional regulation is all about how we process and manage emotion, self-regulation is about how we express those emotions on a behavioral level. Both concepts share neural foundations, but they differ in scope.

Emotional regulation is primarily controlled by the amygdala, which processes emotional stimuli, and the prefrontal cortex, which modulates emotional responses by exerting top-down control (Etkin et al., 2015). This interaction allows for conscious regulation of emotional impulses, helping us maintain composure under stress.

[Self-regulation](https://positivepsychology.com/self-regulation/), on the other hand, extends beyond emotions to include cognitive and behavioral processes, engaging broader neural networks (Langner et al., 2018).

Tracey Marks explains the neuroscience of emotion regulation in her video _The Science of Emotion Regulation: How Our Brains Process Emotions_.

The science of emotion regulation: How our brains process emotions

5 Factors Affecting Emotion Regulation
--------------------------------------

Several factors influence an individual’s ability to regulate emotions (Feldman, 2009).

These internal and external factors interact dynamically, making emotional regulation a complex and multifaceted process that varies widely across individuals and time.

### 1. Genetic factors

Research suggests that genetics plays a role in our predisposition to emotional reactivity and regulation (Hariri & Holmes, 2006). Certain genetic variations can affect neurotransmitter systems, like serotonin, which affects mood and emotional stability (Miu & Bîlc, 2019). With more on this, our article [Is Happiness Genetic?](https://positivepsychology.com/is-happiness-genetic/) looks at the research.

### 2. Physiological factors

The nervous system, particularly the autonomic nervous system (ANS), plays a significant role in emotional regulation (Lang, 2014). For instance, heart rate variability is often associated with better emotion regulation, and chronic stress or trauma can dysregulate the ANS, making it more difficult to regulate our emotions.

### 3. Developmental factors

Developmental factors such as brain development and childhood experiences have been shown to strongly shape our emotional regulation abilities (Thompson, 1991).

Childhood experiences of [secure attachment](https://positivepsychology.com/secure-attachment-style/) foster better emotional coping mechanisms (Thomas et al., 2017). Brain development and plasticity can also have a profound effect on our emotional regulation (Goldsmith et al., 2008).

### 4. Contextual factors

Environmental stressors, such as financial insecurity, relationship difficulties, and workplace challenges, can impair emotional regulation (Aldao & Nolen-Hoeksema, 2012). Conversely, supportive social networks and a stable environment can improve our ability to manage emotions effectively.

### 5. Mental health factors

We know that emotion regulation is important for mental health (Inwood & Ferrari, 2018). But we also know that conditions such as attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, autism spectrum disorder, anxiety, and depression can also make emotion regulation more challenging (Sloan et al., 2017).

Are There Gender Differences in Emotion Regulation?
---------------------------------------------------

While there may be social consensus that there are gender differences in emotional regulation, there hasn’t been much research regarding the specifics of those differences (McRae et al., 2008).

Let’s look at what evidence there is and what it means regarding biology, behavior, and socio-cultural issues.

### Behavioral differences

Women tend to be more likely to [express emotions](https://positivepsychology.com/express-emotions/), while men may use more suppression techniques to control emotional expression, particularly [negative emotions](https://positivepsychology.com/negative-emotions/) like fear or sadness (Collignon et al., 2010). This has been shown to give women an adaptive advantage (Delhom et al., 2021).

### Neural differences

Research has shown that men and women may engage different neural pathways when regulating emotions (Lee et al., 2005). For instance, women tend to engage the left hemisphere, and men tend to activate the right hemisphere more.

### Social & cultural differences

Socialization plays a significant role in emotion regulation (Root & Denham, 2010). Cultural norms and expectations shape how men and women are taught to express and manage emotions. In many societies, men are discouraged from openly displaying vulnerability, while women may be encouraged to be more emotionally expressive.

There are a multitude of factors that play a role in emotion regulation, and even within a specific context like gender, the influence is multifaceted and nuanced. It is important to take cognizance of this in the therapeutic context.
